story:
Everybody knew trolls had been hunted to extinction. It was common knowledge, lumped in with dragons, garden gnomes, and the great auk—one more tragedy smoothed over as human nature. Bridge trolls went first; their teeth fetched absurd prices from people who ignored price tags and morality alike, and with their caretakers gone, the bridges quietly fell into disrepair.

So Elliot had no business seeing a flash of gold beneath a mossy bridge, no business thinking eyes instead of a far-off gaslamp reflected in a puddle. Still, in the rain’s hush—where the world seemed to shrink to stone and water and breath—he wanted to hope. To hope a troll had escaped. To hope people weren’t as awful as they’d proven. It was the last hope he trusted least.

Hope never gave him more than a dull ache, so he checked his watch instead, twirling the chain and wondering if his sister had made it home before the downpour ruined the hair she’d fussed over. The thought almost swept away the impossible. Almost—until he heard a scamper, then a brief scurry, then more scampering. Probably a rat, he told himself. A very heavy, decidedly bipedal rat, as rats so often are. He didn’t fool himself.

Rationally, there couldn’t be a troll under the bridge. Practically, following the noise meant leaving shelter for the rain. Unfortunately for his comfort, Elliot was incurably curious. He tugged his coat tight, raised his collar—if he was going to do something foolish, he refused to be foolish and cold—and ducked into the downpour.

He headed for the smudge of trees left behind by “urban improvement,” the so-called nature park slapped with a feel-better name after everything older was cut down. If he were hiding, he’d choose somewhere one could actually hide; surely a troll, if any had survived, would be wiser than people liked to think themselves.

There was a rustle in the underbrush—something heavier than a rabbit or a cat—moving through the sodden leaves. Another rustle, closer. Leaves betrayed the creature’s path. His breath snagged. Was he about to see something he should not see? Would it hurt him? Would it think he wanted to hurt it? Why him? His thoughts rose like a shaken hornet’s nest, all sting and noise, drowning each other out.

He knelt. “Easy,” he whispered, voice low and sure. “Keep the little one behind you. I won’t hurt either of you.” He parted the wet plants, palms stinging, breath steady, gentled on purpose.

Golden eyes looked back—wide, glassy, terrified. A second pair, smaller, peered up beside them. Understanding passed between them, a quiet thing not made of words.

Against everything, as he’d already known without seeing, the troll was a parent.

Elliot stood at once and let the bush fall closed. He did not see anything, he told himself. He had been silly. He walked stiffly away and did not look back, guarding his lie as if it were a door he could hold shut with both hands—not because he feared being thought foolish, but because some truths stayed safest when they were not said.

After all, everybody knew that trolls had been hunted to extinction years ago.
